sources.name: Data Crystal — Batman: The Video Game (NES)
sources.notes: RAM map referenced but values not extracted in reviewed session.

notes: Batman NES (Sunsoft, 1989) is widely considered one of the best-playing NES action games — tightly designed physics.
DEFINING MECHANIC: Wall jump system. Batman can jump off walls by pressing jump while adjacent to a wall.
The wall jump is more restricted than later wall jump systems (Ninja Gaiden, MMX) — must alternate between two walls.
Regarded as having excellent game feel despite limited exact documentation in public sources.
High walk speed compared to most NES protagonists.
Values below are primarily community estimates. VERIFY against ROM.

walk_speed.notes: Approximate. Batman moves faster than Mega Man (1.5 px/f). Community impression: 'fast and responsive.' VERIFY.

notes: Batman has full horizontal air control during jump.
Jump physics are regarded as 'tight and responsive' by the community.

wall_jump.description: Batman can jump between two parallel walls by pressing jump each time he contacts a wall. Cannot repeatedly jump off the SAME wall.
notes: The wall jump requires TWO walls — Batman must alternate between left and right walls to ascend.
This differs from Ninja Gaiden (same wall cling) and MMX (same wall repeated).
Makes vertical shaft traversal possible but requires proper spacing of walls.
The constraint (must alternate) shapes level design — vertical sections need pairs of walls, not single pillars.

batarang.description: Thrown weapon that travels in a straight horizontal path.
batarang.notes: Limited ammo. Collectible to restore. Fast projectile.

melee.description: Punch and kick combo. Multi-hit enemies require melee.
melee.notes: Melee has short range but is always available.

batman_standing.notes: Batman is taller than most NES protagonists — standard superhero proportions. Hitbox narrower than sprite.

_verification_checklist: Wall jump velocity (horizontal and vertical components)
design_notes_for_giants_drink: Batman NES represents the 'corporate IP done right' school of NES design — a licensed game that uses the IP's strengths (acrobatic hero, urban environment, projectile + melee combat) to inform tight mechanics, not just to slap Batman skin on a generic platformer.

The alternating-walls wall jump creates a distinct rhythm for vertical traversal: left-wall → jump → right-wall → jump → repeat. This rhythm is predictable and teachable, making it feel like a skill rather than a cheap trick.

The combination of full air control + wall jump allows Batman to navigate complex urban environments in ways that feel authentic to the character — no 'Mario parabola' jumps, but fluid directional movement.
Batarang resource management adds a light ammunition economy to what is otherwise pure action — players must decide when to expend ranged attacks vs. risk melee.
